coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots/silicone rubber composites for fluorescence detection of biodiesel in fuel blends
coredata.dc:creator: Koken, Emre
Bulbul Sonmez, Hayal
coredata.dc:description: Carbon-based nanoparticles, specifically carbon quantum dots (CQDs), have received growing interest significantly owing to their optical characteristics and production simplicity. Their diverse surface functionality and media solubility have been used to demonstrate their electronic, biological, and chemical applications. CQDs polymer composites have been widely exhibited in the case of functional applications in related areas by using CQDs’ unique properties. On the detection applications of biofuels, numerous spectroscopic and instrumental approaches have been developed in the literature. Here, organic soluble r-CQDs (QY: 72%) have been synthesized to fabricate fluorescent cross-linked silicone composites that are capable of sensing the biodiesel amount in fuel mixtures by solvatochromic shifts. The fluorescent rubber composites were tested with various degrees of biodiesel blends in 24 h for maximal swelling, and approximately 70% absorption was achieved. The capacity to distinguish biodiesel levels has been effectively revealed with an error level of 2.9 nm at the photoluminescence spectral peak, whereas biodiesel levels were as low as 10%. The colorimetric shift of the swollen composites were also sufficient to identify the biodiesel content visually under UV lights.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biodiesel sensor
Fluorescent composites
Carbon quantum dots
Composites
Solvatochromic shift

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optical results of SylCD-1, SylCD-2%, SylCD-3%, and SylCD-4% in BD0, BD10, and BD20 blends at 24 h (Normalized).
Fuel blend absorption results of SylCD-3% composites over time.
Emission peak shifts for; a) BD0, b) BD10, c) BD20, d) BD50, e) BD100 from 1 to 24 h, f) all blend results at 24 h (Normalized).
Optical peak positions for all SylCD-3% composite of each hour during fuel blend absorption tests.

SylCD-3% composite visuals after from 1 to 24 h of absorption, a-f under UV lights 365 nm, g under 254 nm, and h under day light (from left to right: BD0, BD10, BD20, BD50, and BD100) (background squares are 0.5 × 0.5 cm).

head.ce:title: Carbon dots/silicone rubber composites for fluorescence detection of biodiesel in fuel blends

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Gebze Technical University, Department of Chemistry, 41400, Gebze, Kocaeli, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Gebze Technical University, Department of Chemistry, 41400, Gebze, Kocaeli, Turkey

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon-based nanoparticles, specifically carbon quantum dots (CQDs), have received growing interest significantly owing to their optical characteristics and production simplicity. Their diverse surface functionality and media solubility have been used to demonstrate their electronic, biological, and chemical applications. CQDs polymer composites have been widely exhibited in the case of functional applications in related areas by using CQDs’ unique properties. On the detection applications of biofuels, numerous spectroscopic and instrumental approaches have been developed in the literature. Here, organic soluble r-CQDs (QY: 72%) have been synthesized to fabricate fluorescent cross-linked silicone composites that are capable of sensing the biodiesel amount in fuel mixtures by solvatochromic shifts. The fluorescent rubber composites were tested with various degrees of biodiesel blends in 24 h for maximal swelling, and approximately 70% absorption was achieved. The capacity to distinguish biodiesel levels has been effectively revealed with an error level of 2.9 nm at the photoluminescence spectral peak, whereas biodiesel levels were as low as 10%. The colorimetric shift of the swollen composites were also sufficient to identify the biodiesel content visually under UV lights.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanoparticles have gained increasing attention as an emerging new material for considerably broad-ranging potential applications. Photoluminescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have received a share of this attraction due to their optical properties such as tunable band-edge emissions, larger stoke shifts, controlled ways of fabrication, high solubility in organic solvents, small sizes, and ease of surface functionality modification. By using these properties, their electronic (LEDs, solar cells) [1–3], biological (bioimaging, drug delivery, phototherapy) [4–8], and chemical (sensing, sensors, photo-catalysis) [9–11] applications have been demonstrated. Although CQDs can be produced by both top-down and bottom-up methods, bottom-up synthesis is more versatile with their relatively high conversions and ease of optical property control by the possibility to introduce heteroatoms [12–15]. A commonly used method to produce various photoluminescence colors is the thermal treatment of small molecules [16,17]. Organic molecules are fused and carbonized under rather high temperatures and pressures by using hydrothermal or solvothermal conditions.

1,3,6-trinitropyrene (TNP) is one of the commonly used precursors of both aqueous and solvent based CQDs. –NO2 functionality of TNP produces a N- and O- doped carbon core and surface groups that contribute to the solubility or dispersion of CQDs in a media. The hydrothermal synthesis of –OH functionalized CQDs can be obtained by the de-nitration of TNP via alkaline conditions [18]. Changing the type [1] and amount of alkaline leads to diverse optical results. Heteroatom doping could be done by using thiourea (N&S-doping) [12], 3-mercaptopropionic acid (S-doping) [13], or borax (B-doping) [14] precursors with TNP in a one-pot autoclave synthesis. TNP based CQDs could be useful for fluorescence sensing of cations [13,14] photodynamic therapy (PDT), photothermal therapy (PTT), and bioimaging applications [5–8].

Polymers create a physical environment for CQDs by using them in their synthesis or simply mixing them after synthesis. It has been observed that polymers provide physical and photostability to CQDs, and even escalate their optical properties via surface passivation. For this purpose, polymers have become the preferred reactants for the preparation of CQDs due to their known macromolecular structures [19,20]. Many studies have been carried out by the addition of CQDs to the structure of the polymer gels, which generally swell but do not dissolve under the influence of solvents, and their possible applications including bio or environmental have been demonstrated widely [21–23]. Specifically, Sylgard 184 elastomer kit is commonly used for commercial production of electronic applications due to its superb mechanical properties and simple curing process. Likewise the commercial use, Sylgard 184 is valuable for various fields of science i.e. electronics [24–26], materials science [27–31], mechanobiology [32], biomaterials [33], microfluidics and microengineering [25,27,28].

Different instrumental methods and approaches have been developed in the literature for determining the percentage of biodiesel in biodiesel-diesel mixtures [34–36]. The level of biofuel in biodiesel-diesel blends can be determined by the UV absorption method [34,37] and mixtures in the range of 0–10% by the fluorescence spectroscopy [36,38]. In the study of determination methyl esters of fatty acids (FAME) level (primary constituent of biodiesel) in diesel mixtures, ethyl cellulose-nile blue composites were used [39]. FAME-diesel mixtures were caused a red shift in the UV absorption of the nile blue dye. The purpose of the use of ethyl cellulose polymer is to trap the nile blue and to allow the mixture to interact with the dye easily and then UV absorption of the composites were measured to determine the FAME amount.

Solvatochromism refers to the change in absorption and emission properties of certain substances in response to solvent polarity variations [40]. The behavior is influenced by surface functional groups, such as carboxyl, amine, and hydroxyl groups, which interact with solvent molecules, altering the carbon dots' electronic structure and optical properties [41]. Solvent polarity affects solvation energy and dipole moments, leading to redshifted emission spectra in polar solvents due to charge-transfer interactions. Diesel and biodiesel polarities differ from each other due to the molecular compositions. Diesel is composed of aliphatic and aromatic hydrocarbons, containing only carbon and hydrogen, while biodiesel is composed of ester functional aliphatic chains that contain oxygen and nitrogen besides carbon and hydrogen [42]. The composition and functional group difference creates a polarity contrast between these fuels. The potential solvatochromic shift of the carbon dots can be useful for the detection of biodiesel in fuel blends.

Herein this paper, the aim is to develop fluorescent rubber composites that can detect the biodiesel level in fuel blends by utilizing the solvatochromic property of the CQDs. By using the solvothermal treatment of TNP as a precursor in xylene media, high quantum yield (QY: 72%) red carbon quantum dots (r-CQDs) were obtained. r-CQDs were combined 3% by weight with the silicone elastomer kit (with a prepolymer to cross-linker ratio of 10:1) to produce fluorescent CQD-polymer composites in a rather low curing temperature (Scheme 1 ). The resulting composites were tested with different levels of biodiesel-diesel mixtures for the swelling behaviors over 24 h and ∼70% absorption of mixtures was achieved. Detectability of biodiesel levels is also successfully demonstrated (with 2.9 nm average standard deviation in peak positions) with the change of fluorescent properties over time, while biodiesel levels were as low as 10%. The distinction of the composite colors after 24 h of absorption can be observed under UV lights visually.

Pyrene (98%), nitric acid (65%), sulfuric acid (98%), xylene (mixtures of isomers, 98%), and dichloromethane (DCM) (99%) acquired from Sigma-Aldrich. Sylgard™ 184 silicone elastomer kit were purchased from Dow. Biodiesel and diesel obtain from Opet Petrolcülük A.Ş. All chemicals were from commercial sources and used without any further purification. The water used in all the experiments is ultrapure (MDM Dream Pure) water.

Fluorescence measurements were collected on an Edinburgh Instruments FS5 Spectrofluorometer, using 2 nm slit widths for both excitation and emission. The UV–Vis absorption spectra were carried out by a Thermo Fisher Scientific GENESYS™ 180 UV–Vis Spectrophotometer using quartz cuvettes with 1 cm path length. XPS analysis were acquired on a Thermo Scientific™ K-Alpha X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer (XPS) System. FTIR analysis was performed on a Thermo Scientific™ Nicolet™ iS™ 10 FTIR Spectrometer. The elemental analysis were obtained using a Thermo Scientific™ FLASH 2000 CHNS/O Analyzers.

1,3,6-Trinitropyrene (TNP) was synthesized by modifying a previously reported method [3,46]. Briefly, in a 100 mL round-bottom flask, pyrene (2 g) was initially mixed with 60 mL of 65% nitric acid (HNO3) and then 20 mL of 98% sulfuric acid (H2SO4) was added onto the mixture. The flask was heated to 85 °C in an oil bath for 24 h and let cooled down naturally. The colloidal mixture was poured into 2 L of ultrapure water, filtered and washed until it reached pH 5. The final powder was dried in a vacuum oven at 55 °C for at least 24 h. 1% (by weight) homogenous TNP solution was prepared in xylene using an ultrasonic bath, then the solution was transferred into stainless steel autoclave reactor. The reactor was placed in an oven at 180 °C for 12 h. After the reactor was cooled down to room temperature naturally, r-CQD solution was obtained by purification through 0.22 μm microporous PTFE filter.

7 g of Sylgard 184 and 0.7 g of cross-linker (SylCD-10:1) were mixed with 0.25 g of r-CQD solution and transferred into 5 × 5 cm Teflon mold for curing. The mixture was pre-cured for 15 min at 80 °C and post-cured at 35 °C for 24 h (Scheme 1). The cross-linking degree was modified by changing the cross-linker amount at a ratio of 7:1 (SylCD-7:1). r-CQD concentration in the composites was varied by mixing different volumes of r-CQD solution (SylCD-1%, −2%, −3%, −4%). All of the prepared composites were tested for soluble fraction (SF). SF of the composites was removed by using DCM solvent, on a shaker for 24 h three times.

For the absorption tests, different levels of biodiesel were blended with diesel, labelled as BD0, BD10, BD20, BD50, and BD100 which are 0%, 10%, 20%, 50%, and 100% of biodiesel content, respectively. ∼1 × 1 cm pieces of composites were added into 10 mL blends and shaken via a shaker for 24 h. At designated time intervals (1-2-5-8-22-24 h), the sample absorption percentages were recorded by measuring the weight change of the samples. For each interval, samples’ fluorescence spectra was measured for optical characterization (Scheme 2 ).

TNP is a well-known precursor for hydrothermally or solvothermally produced CQDs [3,14]. In non-polar solvents like toluene or xylene, it is easily dissolved to produce clear solutions of N- and O- doped CQDs. For organic soluble TNP-CQDs, first step is the nitration of pyrene. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and elemental analysis were performed to examine the surface functional groups of TNP to confirm that pyrene was nitrated. For FTIR spectra (Fig. S1), TNP shows different spectroscopic characteristics from pyrene. There are new characteristic strong peaks emerging at about 1521 cm−1 (N–O) and 1335 cm−1 (C–N) for TNP, which can be attributed to the formation of –NO2. Elemental analysis (Table S1) also indicates the presence of –NO2.

Additionally, XPS surveys were performed to further determine the chemical structure of r-CQDs. In the XPS spectra (Fig. S2), there are three major peaks for C 1s, N 1s and O 1s peaks at ∼284.5, ∼400 and ∼532 eV, respectively. The quantitative analysis of the peaks indicate a distribution of 78.45% of C, 1.91% of N, and 19.64% of O, respectively. A high content of C is expected with the graphitization of the precursor via a high temperature solvothermal reaction. The C 1s spectrum peak analysis displays the presence of a graphite sp2 at ∼284.5 eV, which is accompanied by functional groups such as C–O and CN at ∼286.3 and 287.5 eV, respectively. When the N 1s spectrum is investigated in high resolution, pyridinic N at ∼399.4 eV and pyridinic oxide (N–O) at ∼405.9 eV are observed. Thus, the existence of C–N bonds in the high-resolution C 1s spectrum is confirmed by the N 1s spectrum, suggesting that –NO2 pendants of TNP merged into the graphitic carbon frame. Thus, under high temperature and pressure, –NO2 pendants is introduced into r-CQD as pyridinic N or N–O and XPS confirms that it is on the borders of the structure [15,47]. Likewise, the high resolution O 1s spectrum indicates two peaks that are attributed to the functional groups of CO/O–H at ∼532.1 eV and C–O/N–O at ∼533.6 eV. Elemental analysis (Table S2) results further supports the functional groups that XPS data indicates.

Optical characterizations of r-CQDs were done by using UV–Vis spectrophotometer and PL spectrofluorometer (Fig. S3). UV–Vis absorption spectrum shows two major absorbance bands at 417 nm and 518 nm. Although the PL excitation spectrum indicates multiple excitation wavelengths for r-CQDs, 524 nm peak points out the major excitation point, parallel to the UV absorption spectrum as expected. PL emission spectrum, which was collected at the 520 nm excitation wavelength, displays 608 nm peak point with a full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 85 nm. Fig. S3 also shows the excitation dependent emission results of r-CQDs as a color map, indicating that r-CQDs have excitation independent emission.

Sylgard 184 and the cross-linker ratio has been widely investigated for its mechanical properties [32]. The 10:1 (PDMS:curing agent) ratio of the components is commonly used for preparing cross-linked silicone rubbers, as it is prepared in this work (SylCD-10:1). Before curing and molding the 10:1 mixture of components, a previously synthesized and characterized r-CQD solution was added with the amount of 3% (SylCD-3%) by weight. The silicone elastomer kit can be cross-linked at a wide range of curing temperature and time [30]. When the r-CQD containing composite mixture was well dispersed and molded, pre-curing and final-curing was carried out at 80 °C for 15 min and 35 °C for 24 h, respectively. The pre-curing process provided the cross-linking polymerization to initiate and the final curing process certified that all the monomers and/or active sites were consumed.

Polymeric network was intended to support and capture the r-CQD particles. For a better containment of the particles, the ratio of components (Sylgard 184:cross-linker) was modified to 7:1 where cross-linker amount was increased compared to 10:1 ratio [43]. By changing the Sylgard 184:cross-linker ratio from 10:1 to 7:1 resulted in the increase of the cross-linker content in the network. Thus, PDMS (Sylgard 184) chains were bridged with more connections in between [44,45].

The SF results of the two composites, SylCD-10:1 and SylCD-7:1, showed insignificant differences (Table S3). On the other hand, the absorption capacity of two composites (Fig. S4) indicated that increasing the cross-linking density decreases the absorption capacity by 3–9% at 24 h, as expected, depending on the biodiesel content. Thus, the 10:1 ratio is more suitable for this purpose due to its increased absorption capacity and similar containment to the higher cross-linked composite.

For a better resolution of the optical shift, different amounts of r-CQDs were doped to the polymer matrix to investigate the optimum r-CQD % which can provide the maximum optical shift. It was modified from 1% to 4% of r-CQD concentration and containing composites were synthesized (SylCD-1%, −2%, −3%, −4%). Fuel blend absorption test was done with BD0, BD10, and BD20 biodiesel-fuel mixtures for these composites, since these three mixtures were much more similar in optical results. After 24 h of absorption, fluorescence spectroscopy results, Fig. 1
, demonstrated that optical shifts are altered for each composite. SylCD-4% and SylCD-1% showed almost no shift for each blend, while SylCD-2% and SylCD-3% were ∼5 and ∼10 nm apart, respectively. 1% CDs is not enough to detect even the lowest biodiesel levels because the number of biodiesel molecules was much more than the CQDs amount (considering surface groups where the solvatochromic shift occurs). For the 4% CQDs, it is the other way around, meaning, CQDs quantity is too much to be modified or affected by the biodiesel molecules and so unaffected CQDs response is dominant on the PL mechanism, on the spectra. Considering the results, the maximum optical shift was obtained with 10 nm for the SylCD-3% and the optimal r-CQD concentration was determined to be 3%.

Absorption tests were applied for 24 h to each composite to swell maximally. For the absorption study, BD10, BD20, and BD50 diesel-biodiesel blends were prepared volumetrically. BD0 and BD100 were pure diesel and biodiesel, respectively. ∼1 × 1 cm samples of composites were weighted at 1, 2, 5, 8, 22, and 24 h to observe their absorption capacity. Fig. 2 shows the absorption capacity change of SylCD-3% composites over time. It was found that the silicone network structure absorbs biodiesel poorly, while the absorption capacity increases with a decrease of biodiesel composition in the blends. For each hour, BD0, BD10, and BD20 blends display similar absorption levels and the absorption capacities reach almost 70%.

The optical characterizations for each sample were done by fluorescence spectroscopy at the designated time intervals (1-2-5-8-22-24 h). As the absorption level increase, the optical data of every sample indicates a blue shift (hypsochromic) as shown in Fig. 3 . The solvatochromic shift of fluorescence peaks is parallel to the absorption level of the composites, meaning that the more the composite absorbs, the more the peak shifts [40]. On the other hand, the biodiesel content also affects how the peak moves. Pure diesel peak shifts from 655 nm to 614 nm (from 1 h to 24 h), while the unadulterated biodiesel peak changes only 13 nm. As the aforementioned relationship of BD0, BD10, and BD20 absorptions, the optical shift of the blends shows similarity as well. The composite samples tested with BD10 and BD20 mixtures show 29 nm and 22 nm shifts, from 1 h to 24 h respectively.

Fig. 4 shows peak shifts of each composite according to biodiesel blending ratios during 24 h of absorption. From 1 h to 24 h, PL peaks of composites shifted through lower wavelengths. Fig. 3f demonstrates final (24 h) peak results of each composites, BD0 is being the most shifted while BD100 is on the far right-side of the spectrum. In Fig. 4, optical shift of composites in every mixture displays similar and linear behavior with a different rate of change for each blend. Hourly and mixture-wise comparisons of all measurements are given in Fig. 4, PL peaks are shown as a stack plot of 3D bars. From the detailed data, hourly comparison indicates that the peak positions of the blends (at the same hour) shows an increase in wavelength difference between. When this data is linearly fitted, as given in Fig. S5, an obvious decline in slopes can be observed. Similarly, when this comparison is done in linear fits of each blend along the time axis, the slope decreases as BD percentage increases, as shown in Fig. S6. All mathematical equations of linear fit are shown below in Fig. S5 and Fig. S6, demonstrating that the intermediate blending of BD results can be estimated by using the linear fit approach when composites are tested.

Fig. 5 shows the visual results of the composites for each hour of absorption of blends, a-f under 365 nm and g under 254 nm UV and h under day lights. As it is discussed above, with the increasing absorption level of blends optical results are shifting in a different pace. Appearance of the composites alter chromatically during the 24 h and the maximum color distinction in between is at the final hour, as expected. At the end of the tests, composites of BD50 and BD100 show bright, orange, and darker colors and they are visually distinctive than BD0, BD10, and BD20. On the other hand, BD0, BD10, and BD20 displays chromatically analogous appearances. Since biodiesel contents in these mixtures are relatively close, slight differences in appearance of the composites under UV light are expected. Although the distinction between composites might be difficult to observe on images, BD20 composite indicates a bit brighter pinkish color while BD0 composite is being the palest one among the three composites to the bare eye.

In summary, fluorescent and cross-linked silicone composites that are capable of 70% fuel absorption have been successfully developed to identify biodiesel content in fuel mixtures. Composites were designed as fluorescent sensors using solvatochromic effect for detection. Two different cross-linking degrees of composites and four different concentrations of CQDs were investigated to optimize the maximum level of distinction in between the mixtures of BD. Optimum composite, SylCD-3%, has a swelling capacity of 70% at the end of 24 h absorption and it is capable of distinguishing 10% BD optically, with an error of 2.9 nm. Swollen composites can also be differentiated visually. For the intermediate levels of BD in mixtures, BD percentages can be estimated with linear fits of tested levels.

Emre Koken: Conceptualization, Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Hayal Bulbul Sonmez: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledged Quantag Nanotechnologies for their support.
